title: Constant of Proportionality Calculator (y = kx)
tagline: Find the constant of proportionality k in a direct proportion relationship y = kx
intro: This tool helps you calculate the constant of proportionality (k) from one or more pairs of variables (x, y) that are in a direct proportional relationship (y = kx). Add multiple pairs to verify consistency.

error.invalid: Please enter at least one valid (x, y) pair where x ≠ 0.

result.heading: Result
result.consistent: All pairs share the same constant — proportional relationship confirmed.
result.inconsistent: Warning: k values differ between pairs — relationship may not be directly proportional.

about.heading: About Constant of Proportionality Calculator
about.body:
The constant of proportionality (k) describes the ratio between two quantities that are in a direct proportion relationship. When we say y is directly proportional to x, we mean y = k × x, where k is a constant value that remains the same for every corresponding pair of (x, y). The constant k tells us the rate at which y changes per unit increase in x.

To find k, simply divide y by x: k = y / x. If you have multiple data pairs, each pair should produce the same k value — that is the defining test of a direct proportional relationship. If the k values are not equal, the relationship is not directly proportional, though it may be another type of relationship such as inverse proportion (y = k/x), power relationship, or linear with a non-zero intercept (y = mx + b).

The constant of proportionality appears throughout science and everyday life. Ohm's Law states that voltage V = I × R, where R (resistance) is the constant of proportionality between current and voltage. In chemistry, density ρ = m / V is the proportionality constant between mass and volume. In physics, F = m × a means acceleration and force are proportional with mass as the constant. In finance, if the exchange rate is fixed, the amounts in two currencies are proportional.

Graphically, a direct proportion y = kx always passes through the origin (0, 0), and k is the slope of that line. A steeper line has a larger k, meaning y grows faster relative to x. If k > 0, both variables increase together (positive correlation); if k < 0, one increases as the other decreases (inverse linear relationship).

This calculator accepts multiple (x, y) pairs and computes k = y/x for each pair. If all pairs yield the same k, the tool confirms the proportional relationship. It also displays the equation y = k × x so you can use the constant to predict y for any new x value, or x for any new y value.

howto.heading: How to Use the Constant of Proportionality Calculator
steps: Enter the X value and Y value for the first data pair. X must be non-zero (division by zero is undefined).
Click 'Add Pair' to add more (x, y) pairs if you want to verify that multiple data points share the same constant.
Click 'Calculate' to compute k = y/x for each pair and display the proportionality equation y = k × x.
Check whether all k values are consistent. If they match, the data confirms a direct proportional relationship. If not, the relationship may not be y = kx.
Use the quick-load example buttons to explore real-world proportionality scenarios from physics, cooking, and finance.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is the constant of proportionality?
faq.q1_a: The constant of proportionality k is the fixed ratio k = y/x in the relationship y = kx. It represents the rate of change: for every 1 unit increase in x, y increases by k units. The value of k can be any non-zero real number — positive, negative, or fractional. It is also known as the unit rate in many applied contexts.
faq.q2: How do I know if two variables are directly proportional?
faq.q2_a: Two variables are directly proportional if (1) their ratio y/x is the same for every data point, and (2) when x = 0, y = 0. You can verify this by computing k = y/x for each (x, y) pair — if all values are equal (or nearly equal given rounding), the variables are directly proportional. Graphically, the data points should all lie on a straight line through the origin.
faq.q3: What is the difference between direct and inverse proportion?
faq.q3_a: In direct proportion (y = kx), as x increases, y increases proportionally — the ratio y/x is constant. In inverse proportion (y = k/x), as x increases, y decreases — the product x × y is constant. For example, speed and travel time at a fixed distance are inversely proportional: double the speed and you halve the time.
faq.q4_a: Yes. A negative k means y and x change in opposite directions. For example, if a car loses 0.5 litres of fuel per kilometre, then fuel remaining F = F₀ − 0.5d. The proportional part (change in fuel) = −0.5 × (distance), so k = −0.5. This is a valid constant of proportionality for the incremental relationship.
faq.q5: Why must x not equal zero?
faq.q5_a: The formula k = y/x requires dividing by x. Division by zero is mathematically undefined. In the proportional relationship y = kx, when x = 0, y must also equal 0 regardless of k — so the origin (0, 0) contains no information about k. Always use non-zero x values to determine the constant.
faq.q6: How is the constant of proportionality used in science?
faq.q6_a: It appears in virtually every area of science: Ohm's Law (V = IR, R is the proportionality constant), Hooke's Law (F = kx, k is spring stiffness), Newton's second law (F = ma, m is the proportionality constant between force and acceleration), Beer-Lambert Law in chemistry (absorbance = ε × c × l), and gravitational force between two masses (F = G × m₁m₂/r²). Identifying the constant of proportionality is often the first step in modelling a physical system.
